EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak low pressure system moved southeast of southern New England, bringing snow to much of southern New England.  While most of the area received at least an inch of snow, a mesoscale band set up along the south coast of Massachusetts and Rhode Island resulting in incredible snowfall rates.  Eight to twelve inches of snow fell along the coast with five to eight inches falling on Martha's Vineyard and Nantucket.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Amateur Radio operator reported 3 to 5 inches of snow on the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved across southern New England resulting in gusty winds.  Some of these winds did some minor damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak low pressure passing south of New England brought a wintry mix of precipitation to Massachusetts. Light freezing rain in northern Massachusetts resulted in a light coating of ice, with up to 1/4 inch of glaze reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 1/4 inch of icing brought down tree limbs in Westminster and Shrewsbury.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved up the east coast spreading snow, rain, and wind across southern New England. Cloudy skies coupled with evaporational cooling to keep temperatures cooler than expected which resulted in snow spread across all but the south coasts of RI and MA as well as portions of southeastern MA. This in turn resulted in higher snow accumulations across much of southern New England. In Massachusetts, accumulations ranged from less than an inch to nine inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observing System at Nantucket Memorial Airport recorded sustained wind speeds of 51 mph.  An amateur radio operator reported wind gusts of 63 mph.  No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved across Southern New England bringing a period of snow to the western parts of Massachusetts and New Hampshire.  Two to five inches of snow fell across this area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to five inches of snow fell across Western Franklin County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved across Southern New England bringing a period of snow to the western parts of Massachusetts and New Hampshire.  Two to five inches of snow fell across this area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to six inches of snow fell across Western Hampshire county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure across the Great Lakes and high pressure southeast of New England resulted in the strengthening of low level winds across southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observing Station at Blue Hill Observatory and Science Center in Milton, MA (KMQE) recorded sustained wind speeds up to 43 mph and gusts to 63 mph.  No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure across the Great Lakes and high pressure southeast of New England resulted in the strengthening of low level winds across southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observing System at New Bedford Regional Airport (KEWB) recorded sustained wind speeds of 37 mph and gusts to 53 mph.  In addition, amateur radio operators reported that a gas station canopy at the intersection of Shawmut Avenue and Durfee Streets collapsed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moving across New England induced the development of an inverted trough along the east coast of Massachusetts.  Strong low level convergence in this region resulted in snow falling, mainly along the coast, across eastern Massachusetts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to five inches of snow fell across portions of coastal Essex County, with most of the snow focused on the Cape Ann region.  A ten vehicle crash occurred on the bridge between Salem and Beverly when a tractor trailer jack-knifed.  A local newspaper reported that the snow was to blame for the accident.  Two people were transported to a local hospital.

In Dartmouth, a tree was downed at the intersection of Kingston Street and Hathaway Road.  A tree was downed on wires leading to a house.

In Wellfleet, several trees were downed, resulting in scattered power outages.  A tree was downed onto wires in Barnstable.  A tree was downed on Service Road near the gun range.  A large tree was downed onto a house in Bourne.  In Falmouth, several trees were downed, including near Woods Hole Road and Leslie Street.  A tree was downed across Crows Pond Road in Chatham.  In Orleans, a tree was down across Forest Way.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observing System at Martha's Vineyard Airport (KMVY) recorded sustained wind speeds of 46 mph and wind gusts of 62 mph.  No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deepening low pressure moving across Maine resulted in strong and damaging winds across southern New England.  Trees were downed across much of eastern Massachusetts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observing System (ASOS) at Barnstable Municipal-Boardman Airport (KHYA) in Hyannis recorded sustained wind speeds of 36 mph and gusts to 48 mph.  The ASOS at Chatham Municipal Airport (KCQX) recorded gusts to 41 mph.  In addition, amateur radio operators recorded wind gusts of 59 and 60 mph on their home weather stations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deepening low pressure moving across Maine resulted in strong and damaging winds across southern New England.  Trees were downed across much of eastern Massachusetts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observing System at Hanscom Field (KBED) in Bedford recorded sustained wind speeds of 35 mph and gusts to 47 mph.  In addition, trees were downed in Newton, Natick, and Concord.  One of the trees downed in Newton landed on a house on Blake Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deepening low pressure moving across Maine resulted in strong and damaging winds across southern New England.  Trees were downed across much of eastern Massachusetts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observing System at Worcester Regional Airport (KORH) recorded sustained wind speeds of 38 mph and gusts to 58 mph.  In addition, trees were downed in Worcester and Grafton.   Downed trees in Worcester fell on two cars.

In addition, an amateur radio operator recorded wind gusts of 60 mph on their home weather station.  Trees were downed in Brookline and Wellesley.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observing System at Logan International Airport (KBOS) in Boston recorded sustained wind speeds of 41 mph and gusts to 53 mph.  In addition, several trees were downed in Boston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The U.S. Drought Monitor declared a severe drought across Rhode Island, the eastern half of Massachusetts, and most of northern Connecticut. A moderate drought was declared over western Massachusetts and southwestern New Hampshire. This was declared as the result of a meteorological drought determined by precipitation that had been approximately one half of normal from January 2012 through April 2012. Rivers and streams were most affected as most ran at record low levels during the spring run-off season. No southern New England state issued drought declarations as reservoirs were at normal levels, thanks largely to above normal precipitation falling between August 2011 and November 2011. ||The main impact of the meteorological drought was periods of very high fire danger. In addition, small pond levels were reduced. While soil moisture was well below normal, this drought occurred prior to the beginning of the growing season. Thus, no agricultural impacts were realized. ||From January 1 through April 15, precipitation levels were 6 to 8 inches below normal across northeast Connecticut, Rhode Island, and southeast Massachusetts. Across the remainder of southern New England, precipitation levels were 5 to 7 inches below normal. This translates to around or less than 50 percent of normal precipitation for much of southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The U.S. Drought Monitor declared severe drought (D2) over southern Bristol County from April 12 through May 15. This was deemed a meteorological drought due to precipitation levels approximately one half of normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High spring astronomical tides combined with low pressure off the east coast of Massachusetts to produce coastal flooding. This unseasonable coastal low pressure contributed to the flooding lasting through multiple tide cycles. Accompanying the low pressure was rainfall that further intensified the flooding, with the coastal contribution making it difficult for freshwater flooding to drain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tenth Street in Salisbury was flooded for 100 feet with seawater.  Essex Avenue in Gloucester was flooded to the edge of the street.  Causeway Street in Gloucester was flooded with a few inches of seawater.  This was all considered minor coastal flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High spring astronomical tides combined with low pressure off the east coast of Massachusetts to produce coastal flooding. This unseasonable coastal low pressure contributed to the flooding lasting through multiple tide cycles. Accompanying the low pressure was rainfall that further intensified the flooding, with the coastal contribution making it difficult for freshwater flooding to drain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lighthouse Road, Bailey's Causeway, and Cole Parkway in Scituate were closed due to moderate coastal flooding.  Seventh and Central Avenues, Edwards Foster, Oceanside, Turner, Rebecca, Glades, and Surfside Roads, Town Way, and Seagate Circle were all flooded and impassable.  Rebecca and 7th Roads had a significant amount of overwash material in the road the morning of June 5.  The parking lot along Old Dock Street was flooded with a foot of water.  Three average size cars were in water up to the car doors.  In Marshfield, Plymouth Avenue and Dyke Road were closed.  The parking lot at Hingham Harbor was flooded.  In Hull, Moreland Avenue was closed due to flooding.  V Street and Sunset Avenue were flooded.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The intersection of Forest Street and Trapelo Road was flooded and impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The U.S. Drought Monitor declared a severe drought across Rhode Island, the eastern half of Massachusetts, and most of northern Connecticut. A moderate drought was declared over western Massachusetts and southwestern New Hampshire. This was declared as the result of a meteorological drought determined by precipitation that had been approximately one half of normal from January 2012 through April 2012. Rivers and streams were most affected as most ran at record low levels during the spring run-off season. No southern New England state issued drought declarations as reservoirs were at normal levels, thanks largely to above normal precipitation falling between August 2011 and November 2011. ||The main impact of the meteorological drought was periods of very high fire danger. In addition, small pond levels were reduced. While soil moisture was well below normal, this drought occurred prior to the beginning of the growing season. Thus, no agricultural impacts were realized. ||From January 1 through April 15, precipitation levels were 6 to 8 inches below normal across northeast Connecticut, Rhode Island, and southeast Massachusetts. Across the remainder of southern New England, precipitation levels were 5 to 7 inches below normal. This translates to around or less than 50 percent of normal precipitation for much of southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The U.S. Drought Monitor declared severe drought (D2) over northwest Middlesex County from April 12 through May 15. This was deemed a meteorological drought due to precipitation levels approximately one half of normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High spring astronomical tides combined with low pressure off the east coast of Massachusetts to produce coastal flooding. This unseasonable coastal low pressure contributed to the flooding lasting through multiple tide cycles. Accompanying the low pressure was rainfall that further intensified the flooding, with the coastal contribution making it difficult for freshwater flooding to drain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Commercial Street in Salem was flooded from end to end with 21 inches of water at its deepest point.  Several inches of water flooded Lee Fort Terrace.  Columbus Avenue was flooded with six to seven inches of water.  Water flowed over the wall at Columbus Avenue and ran down Bay View Avenue toward Salem Willows.  In Marblehead, a parking lot near Devereaux Beach was flooded with splashover reaching the Marblehead Causeway.  Lynn Shore Drive on the Lynn/Swampscott line was flooded with small rocks 2 inches in diameter washed onto the road.  Also in Lynn, splashover at Nahant Street and Lynn Shore Drive resulted in small debris washed into the roads.  In Salisbury, overwash moved over the intersection of North End Boulevard and down Beach Road almost to the entrance to Reservation Road.  Liberty Street was flooded and impassable due to moderate coastal flooding.  There was significant beach erosion in both Salisbury and Newburyport.  In Gloucester, Causeway Street was completely flooded with seawater.  Tide washed over Jeffrey's Neck Road in Ipswich.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure offshore brought warm southwesterly flow over southern New England.  Above normal temperatures combined with low dewpoints resulting in relative humidity levels in the 15 to 25 percent range.  These conditions along with gusty winds at times contributed to the spread of a fire in meadowlands in Dedham.  Firefighters were unable to determine the initial cause of the fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nearly 100 acres of meadow land burned on the Dedham-Boston line near Route 128.  No injuries or structural damage were reported.  Below normal rainfall over the previous 3 to 6 months resulted in dry grasses which fueled the fire.  Most of the flames were extinguished by 8:30pm EST, but firefighters remained on the scene through the night watching for hot spots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken line of thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours ahead on an approaching surface cold front.  The thunderstorms moved from the Taconics on New York into Berkshire County during the early afternoon hours. With a moist atmosphere in place, one of the thunderstorms along the line became severe, with damaging winds.  This line of thunderstorms ended by the mid-afternoon as the line quickly moved eastward into central Massachusetts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down and lying across a railroad track due to thunderstorm winds in Berkshire Heights.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extraordinarily dry conditions combined with winds gusting to 25 to 30 mph to produce conditions ideal for fire spread.  A brush fire in Brimfield moved into an area of blown down debris from the June 1, 2011 tornado and became difficult to control.  In addition, a thunderstorm blew through the area while firefighters were attempting to control the blaze.  For their safety, firefighters were called off the fire until the storm passed.  The fire eventually burned about 50 acres.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brush fire ignited around noon in extraordinarily dry conditions.  The fire moved into an area of blown down debris from the June 1, 2011 tornado and spread quickly.  No structures were destroyed but numerous houses were threatened.  Firefighting operations continued into the morning of April 5 with numerous fire departments from both Massachusetts and Connecticut.  A Black Hawk helicopter from the Army National Guard assisted with water drops.  A total of 50 acres burned.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate coastal flooding impacted Quincy and Weymouth.  The Kennedy Center parking lot at East Squantum Street and Victory Road in Quincy was flooded with several inches of water.  In Weymouth, Fort Point and Wessagusett Roads were flooded with one foot of water and closed.  Fore River Road was flooded with two feet of water.  Neck Street was flooded with several inches of water with water up to one foot deep on a driveway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The U.S. Drought Monitor declared a severe drought across Rhode Island, the eastern half of Massachusetts, and most of northern Connecticut. A moderate drought was declared over western Massachusetts and southwestern New Hampshire. This was declared as the result of a meteorological drought determined by precipitation that had been approximately one half of normal from January 2012 through April 2012. Rivers and streams were most affected as most ran at record low levels during the spring run-off season. No southern New England state issued drought declarations as reservoirs were at normal levels, thanks largely to above normal precipitation falling between August 2011 and November 2011. ||The main impact of the meteorological drought was periods of very high fire danger. In addition, small pond levels were reduced. While soil moisture was well below normal, this drought occurred prior to the beginning of the growing season. Thus, no agricultural impacts were realized. ||From January 1 through April 15, precipitation levels were 6 to 8 inches below normal across northeast Connecticut, Rhode Island, and southeast Massachusetts. Across the remainder of southern New England, precipitation levels were 5 to 7 inches below normal. This translates to around or less than 50 percent of normal precipitation for much of southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The U.S. Drought Monitor declared severe drought (D2) over western Plymouth County from April 12 through May 15. This was deemed a meteorological drought due to precipitation levels approximately one half of normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the heels of a bigger snowstorm, a light to moderate snowfall occurred across the region on Saturday, December 29th, 2012. An area of low pressure developed along the coast of North Carolina and moved northeastward off the Eastern Seaboard. Although the storm passed well to the south, an upper level disturbance associated with the storm allowed for light snow to occur throughout the day. With temperatures cold across the entire area, the precipitation remained entirely in the form of snow. Most areas across the Berkshires reported between 4 and 6 inches of snow.

On the heels of a bigger snowstorm, a light to moderate snowfall occurred across the region on Saturday, December 29th, 2012. An area of low pressure developed along the coast of North Carolina and moved northeastward off the Eastern Seaboard. Although the storm passed well to the south, an upper level disturbance associated with the storm allowed for light snow to occur throughout the day. With temperatures cold across the entire area, the precipitation remained entirely in the form of snow. Most areas across the Berkshires reported between 4 and 6 inches of snow.

A rapidly intensifying low moved out of the mid-Atlantic, passing southeast of Southern New England.  This spread heavy snow across much of Southern New England, resulting in six to ten inches of snow across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to nine inches of snow fell across western Norfolk County.  Heavy snow downed several trees in Brookline, where nearly six inches of snow fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure organized on Christmas Day over the Deep South, producing widespread rain and severe weather across the Gulf Coast Region. As this storm lifted northward on Christmas Night, a secondary area of low pressure developed along the mid-Atlantic coastline. This secondary low become the dominant storm center and moved northeast towards Long Island.  This storm then continued northeast across southeastern New England before lifting towards the Canadian Maritimes.||Snow, heavy at times, fell across much of western Massachusetts from the evening of the 26th into the day on the 27th.  Warmer air moving in aloft allowed for the precipitation to mix with or change over to sleet and freezing rain across mainly southern portions of the Berkshires. Total storm snowfall amounts ranged for just 4 inches in downsloped valley areas to 20 inches in Savoy.  In addition, strong southeast winds were gusty, especially across the higher elevations.  A wind gust to 46 mph was recorded at North Adams and 36 mph at Pittsfield.  This storm resulted in very slow travel during the holiday season, especially on the evening of the 26th and morning on the 27th.

A cold front moving through a very warm, moist airmass over Rhode Island and eastern Massachusetts resulted in showers and thunderstorms. Many of these storms became severe, producing damaging winds and large hail.

Several areas of low pressure traveled along a stationary front stalled across Southern New England producing showers and a few thunderstorms.  In addition, strong southerly flow brought in a very moist airmass with precipitable water values above two inches.  This resulted in very heavy rain falling in these showers and thunderstorms.  Many locations received up to two to three inches in less than an hour.  This resulted in flash flooding, particularly in more urban areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 202 near the Granby line was flooded with six to eight inches of water.

Many locations received up to two to three inches in less than an hour.  This resulted in flash flooding, particularly in more urban areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several basements flooded on Pine View Circle.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: In New Bedford, West Rodney French Boulevard, Cove, Roosevelt, Orchard, Fair, Purchase, Weld, Penniman, and Allen Streets, and Rockdale Avenue were all flooded with 6 to 12 inches of water.  Belleville Avenue between Hatch and Covell Streets was flooded with three feet of water.  Cars were backed up on Route 18 at the Weld Street offramp due to flooding on the ramp.  Basements were flooded on several streets.  A manhole cover was blown off by flooding on Acushnet Avenue.  The Coggeshall Street onramp to Interstate 195 westbound was flooded and impassable.  A funeral home car was stuck in two feet of water on Dartmouth Street.  In Fairhaven, the intersection of Washington and Adams Streets was flooded.

Low pressure moved up the east coast spreading snow, rain, and wind across southern New England. Cloudy skies coupled with evaporational cooling to keep temperatures cooler than expected which resulted in snow spread across all but the south coasts of RI and MA as well as portions of southeastern MA. This in turn resulted in higher snow accumulations across much of southern New England. In Massachusetts, accumulations ranged from less than an inch to nine inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 54 mph were reported by an amateur radio operator in Marion.  Trees on Washburn and Point Roads were downed.  In Rochester, a tree at the intersection of Vaughn Hill Road and High Street was downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front produced showers and thunderstorms, a few of which became severe producing both large hail and gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wires on Court Street were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very complex storm system brought a wintry mix of precipitation to the Berkshires on Thursday, January 12th. Snow, sleet, freezing rain and rain all occurred as the system impacted the region. Snowfall and sleet reports were generally in the 2 to 6 inch range with ice accretions up to around a tenth of an inch.

Rivers and streams were most affected as most ran at record low levels during the spring run-off season. This translates to around or less than 50 percent of normal precipitation for much of southern New England.

A moderate drought was declared over western Massachusetts and southwestern New Hampshire. This was declared as the result of a meteorological drought determined by precipitation that had been approximately one half of normal from January 2012 through April 2012. Rivers and streams were most affected as most ran at record low levels during the spring run-off season. No southern New England state issued drought declarations as reservoirs were at normal levels, thanks largely to above normal precipitation falling between August 2011 and November 2011. ||The main impact of the meteorological drought was periods of very high fire danger. In addition, small pond levels were reduced. While soil moisture was well below normal, this drought occurred prior to the beginning of the growing season. Thus, no agricultural impacts were realized. ||From January 1 through April 15, precipitation levels were 6 to 8 inches below normal across northeast Connecticut, Rhode Island, and southeast Massachusetts. Across the remainder of southern New England, precipitation levels were 5 to 7 inches below normal. This translates to around or less than 50 percent of normal precipitation for much of southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The U.S. Drought Monitor declared severe drought (D2) over eastern Essex County from April 12 through May 1. This was deemed a meteorological drought due to precipitation levels approximately one half of normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving warm front across southern New England resulted in a very moist atmosphere. This resulted in showers and thunderstorms developing across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck the former Gales Newsstand building in Palmer at the corner of Main and Central Streets, setting the building on fire.  The building was lost and the building next door was threatened but saved.  No one was injured.  Damage amount taken from the value of the building from the assessor's office.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwesterly surface flow resulted in warm air advection, bringing plenty of warm, moist air into southern New England.  A cold front approaching from the west provided enough lift to result in showers and thunderstorms across western Massachusetts.  Some of these storms were strong to severe.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large branches on Turners Falls Road were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a 40 foot pile of scrap metal at Mid City Steel in Westport, MA setting it on fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of upper level disturbances rotated around a vertically stacked low pressure system in the Great Lakes.  These provided a focus for showers and thunderstorms to develop across southern New England.  Southerly winds drew tropical moisture over the area, resulting in very heavy rain in showers and thunderstorms that developed.  In addition, strong winds in the upper levels and 30-40 knots of deep layer shear resulted in wind damage with the strongest of these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree and wires were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High spring astronomical tides combined with low pressure off the east coast of Massachusetts to produce coastal flooding. This unseasonable coastal low pressure contributed to the flooding lasting through multiple tide cycles. Accompanying the low pressure was rainfall that further intensified the flooding, with the coastal contribution making it difficult for freshwater flooding to drain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fort Point Road in Weymouth was flooded from Bacon Road to the end was flooded with at least one foot of water.  A large portion of Bacon Road was also flooded with at least a foot of water.  Wessagusett Road from Yacht Club to Parker Street was flooded with a foot of water and impassable.  The seawall has a hole in it on Fore River Avenue near Sea Street, this intersection is flooded with two feet of water and is impassable.  Fore River Avenue was closed due to moderate coastal flooding.  King Cove Beach Road at Beach Road was closed due to flooding near 91 Beach Road.  In Quincy, the 700 block of Sea Street was flooded.  Enterprise Road was flooded and impassable.  The side spur of East Squantum Street to approximately 440 East Squantum Street was flooded and impassable.  At least 30 basements were flooded in Quincy, most of which were along Armory and Lafayette Streets, near Furnace Brook.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor coastal flooding impacted locations throughout Salem and Gloucester.  In Salem, Commercial Street was flooded from end to end with one foot of water.  Bridge Street was flooded with six inches of water, but was passable with water shallower in the center of the street.   In Gloucester, Eastern Point Boulevard was flooded with 4 to 6 inches of water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through a hot and humid southern New England producing showers and thunderstorms. Many of these storms became severe, resulting in damaging winds, large hail, and some flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a tree on 4th Street in Worcester, igniting a fire to the house next to the tree.  Six adults were displaced by the fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High spring astronomical tides combined with low pressure off the east coast of Massachusetts to produce coastal flooding.  This unseasonable coastal low pressure contributed to the flooding lasting through multiple tide cycles.  Accompanying the low pressure was rainfall that further intensified the flooding, with the coastal contribution making it difficult for freshwater flooding to drain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate coastal flooding occurred throughout coastal Plymouth County.  In Hull, waves crossed Hull Shore Drive almost reaching the Red Parrot Restaurant.  In the Sand Hills section of Scituate, there was minor street flooding on 7th Avenue with the road passable.  Lighthouse Road was closed due to 6 inches of water over the road.  Several cars were stuck in flood waters near the Mill Wharf parking lot on Cole Parkway.  Gilson Road was also flooded in the Third Cliff section.  Bailey's Causeway and Front Street were both flooded and impassable.  In Plymouth a car was stuck in floodwaters on Saquish Beach Boulevard.  Three people were rescued from the car, one woman (a grandmother from Georgia) was treated for hypothermia on the scene.  Gurnet Road in Duxbury was flooded and impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance and associated cold pool provided enough lift and instability to produce showers and thunderstorms. In addition, there was enough shear to sustain these thunderstorms. These storms produced damaging wind and some large hail across portions of southern New Hampshire and northeastern Massachusetts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree limbs were downed by thunderstorms throughout Scituate, including one on South Main Street that was downed onto wires.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS storm survey team determined a microburst occurred in Taunton.  Winds were estimated to be 70 to 80 mph. Six maple trees were uprooted on Tremont Street along Cobbs Creek, landing on four vehicles in the driveway behind a house.  Several large branches were snapped and small sections of siding were torn off the house.  A large spruce tree eight inches in diameter was blown onto a house and many other large tree limbs were snapped off trees.  The house sustained minor damage.  Two more trees fell behind this house.  Numerous tree branches were downed.  Behind another house, a tree fell taking down a portion of the back fence.  On Eldridge Street, a silver maple tree about six inches in diameter was snapped near the base and fell onto a house.  Several large branches were downed in this area as well.  A sheet metal shed was blown into the next yard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved up the coast over Southern New England from the mid-Atlantic.  This brought a mixture of wintry precipitation and strong winds to the region. In addition, minor coastal flooding was observed along east-facing shores.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five to ten inches of snow fell across eastern Franklin County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across Southern New England ushering in a period of strong cold air advection.  This produced excellent mixing, allowing stronger winds aloft to mix down to the surface.  Strong to damaging, high winds resulted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observation System at Barnstable Municipal Airport recorded sustained winds at 45 MPH with gusts up to 59 MPH. In addition, a few shingles were blown off of a roof of a house and a tree was down on Long Pond Road. A few amateur radio operators reported gusts up to 70 MPH.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to nine inches of snow fell across western Hampden County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upslope snow occurred across the Berkshires in the wake of an arctic frontal boundary Friday, January 13th. Seven to 12 inches of snow was reported across the northern Berkshires with 5 to 7 inches across the southern Berkshires. Brisk and gusty westerly winds resulted in blowing and drifting of the snow.

Upslope snow occurred across the Berkshires in the wake of an arctic frontal boundary Friday, January 13th. Seven to 12 inches of snow was reported across the northern Berkshires with 5 to 7 inches across the southern Berkshires. Brisk and gusty westerly winds resulted in blowing and drifting of the snow.

Low pressure moved up the east coast spreading snow, rain, and wind across southern New England. Cloudy skies coupled with evaporational cooling to keep temperatures cooler than expected which resulted in snow spread across all but the south coasts of RI and MA as well as portions of southeastern MA. This in turn resulted in higher snow accumulations across much of southern New England. In Massachusetts, accumulations ranged from less than an inch to nine inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph were reported by members of the public.  In Carver, a trees were downed onto wires on High Street and on Old South Meadow Road.  In Whitman, a large tree was downed on Auburn Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably warm and humid air behind a warm front triggered the development of scattered showers and thunderstorms across southeast Massachusetts. Locally heavy rainfall in the urbanized corridor around New Bedford resulted in minor flooding. Rainfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported in New Bedford on parts of Route 18, Purchase Street, and at the intersection of Rockdale, Coggeshall and Sawyer Streets. Route 18 was closed in both directions for a time because of water up to 30 inches deep flooding the roadway.  The I-195 eastbound exit ramp to Route 18 South was closed for a brief time due to water covering the roadway. In Fairhaven, flooding affected Route 6 at Green Street as well as on Bridge and Spring Streets, where as much as 18 inches of water covered the road. Two cars were stranded on Adams Street after attempting to drive through the flooded roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low level jet (65-75 kt) coupled with strong pressure falls produced gusty winds, some of which reached high wind criteria.  With trees already defoliated, damage was fairly minimal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An amateur radio operator in Scituate recorded a gust to 51 mph on their home weather station.  The weatherflow reporting site in Duxbury reported sustained winds of 44 mph.  In addition, a tree was downed onto Route 116 in Scituate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low level jet (65-75 kt) coupled with strong pressure falls produced gusty winds, some of which reached high wind criteria.  With trees already defoliated, damage was fairly minimal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Amateur radio operators recorded wind gusts up to 62 mph on their home weather stations.  The Automated Surface Observation System at Beverly Municipal Airport (KBVY) recorded wind gusts to 49 mph.  No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low level jet (65-75 kt) coupled with strong pressure falls produced gusty winds, some of which reached high wind criteria.  With trees already defoliated, damage was fairly minimal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Weatherflow Deer Island reporting station recorded sustained wind speeds of 46 mph and gusts to 55 mph.  No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low level jet (65-75 kt) coupled with strong pressure falls produced gusty winds, some of which reached high wind criteria.  With trees already defoliated, damage was fairly minimal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One of the anemometers at Blue Hill Observatory in Milton, MA recorded sustained wind speeds of 38 mph and wind gusts to 59 mph.  No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low level jet (65-75 kt) coupled with strong pressure falls produced gusty winds, some of which reached high wind criteria.  With trees already defoliated, damage was fairly minimal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Amateur radio operators reported gusts up to around 55 mph.  The Automated Surface Observing System at New Bedford Regional Airport (KEWB) recorded sustained winds of 33mph and gusts to 46mph.  The Automated Surface Observing System at Taunton Municipal Airport (KTAN) recorded sustained winds of 31mph and gusts to 49mph.  A tree was downed on Route 24 northbound near exit 9 in Assonet.  Trees were downed onto power lines on Hamlin Street in Acushnet.  Wires were downed on Bank Street in Fall River and on North Main Street in Freetown.  Also in Freetown, a large limb was downed onto wires.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deepening low pressure moving across Maine resulted in strong and damaging winds across southern New England.  Trees were downed across much of eastern Massachusetts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observing System at Lawrence Municipal Airport (KLWM) recorded sustained wind speeds of 36 mph and gusts to 53 mph.  In addition, trees were downed in West Newbury and Middleton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several waves of low pressure moved south of southern New England bringing a prolonged period of snow to the region.  Anywhere from 1 to 12 inches of snow fell across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to eight inches of snow fell across eastern Franklin County ending on March 1.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex multi-part long duration (24 to 36 hour) storm blanketed the Berkshires with 3 to 11 inches of snow and sleet on Wednesday, February 29th and Thursday, March 1st. ||A large low pressure system approached from the upper mid west and Great Lakes region Wednesday. In association with the system's warm front, snow initially overspread the area. As warmer air worked its way into the area Wednesday night, the snow transitioned to a wintry mix. A secondary low began to form in the mid-Atlantic region early Thursday morning. This resulted in a double-barrelled low pressure system which moved eastward during the day Thursday. The wintry mix transitioned back to snow which persisted most of the day. However, the snow had trouble accumulating in some locations, especially at lower elevations, as temperatures warmed.

Low pressure across the Great Lakes and high pressure southeast of New England resulted in the strengthening of low level winds across southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observing Station at Hanscom Field in Bedford, MA (KBED) recorded sustained wind speeds of 33 mph and gusts to 46 mph.  In addition, amateur radio operators reported a downed tree on Champa Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak surface convergence ahead of a backdoor cold front, coupled with some instability resulted in a few strong to severe showers and thunderstorms around midnight across portions of northern Massachusetts.  These produced mostly small hail, but there were a few reports of hail up to the size of half dollars.

Showers and thunderstorms developed in a warm, moist atmosphere.  Cold air aloft contributed to an unstable environment resulting in severe thunderstorms.  Moisture was such that rainfall rates were high enough to result in flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One car was stuck at a restaurant on Route 5.  Water was up to the floor boards of another car on Prospect Street.  The Automated Surface Observing System at Barnes Municipal Airport (KBAF) recorded 1.14 inches of rain in 20 minutes and a total of just under 1.50 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed in a warm, moist atmosphere.  Cold air aloft contributed to an unstable environment resulting in severe thunderstorms.  Moisture was such that rainfall rates were high enough to result in flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Interstate 195 eastbound was flooded near the entrance to the Braga Bridge.  Davol Street near Battleship Cove also was flooded with several cars stuck in the waters.  Roosevelt Street was flooded with eight inches of water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After thunderstorms occurred during the early morning hours, clouds cleared allowing for ample sunshine.  This resulted in the atmosphere destabilizing.  A cold front moved through the region producing showers and thunderstorms.  Some of these storms became severe producing damaging winds.  High rainfall rates in a moisture laden atmosphere resulted in flash flooding as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Davol Street was closed due to flooding near the Global Gas Station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sandy, a hybrid storm with both tropical and extra-tropical characteristics, brought high winds and coastal flooding to southern New England.  Easterly winds gusted to 50 to 60 mph for interior southern New England; 55 to 65 mph along the eastern Massachusetts coast and along the I-95 corridor in southeast Massachusetts and Rhode Island; and 70 to 80 mph along the southeast Massachusetts and Rhode Island coasts.  A few higher higher gusts occurred along the Rhode Island coast.  A severe thunderstorm embedded in an outer band associated with Sandy produced wind gusts to 90 mph and concentrated damage in Wareham early Tuesday evening, |a day after the center of Sandy had moved into New Jersey.  In general, moderate coastal flooding occurred along the Massachusetts coastline, and major coastal flooding impacted the Rhode Island coastline.  The storm surge was generally 2.5 to 4.5 feet along the east coast of Massachusetts, but peaked late Monday afternoon in between high tide cycles.  Seas built to between 20 and 25 feet Monday afternoon and evening just off the Massachusetts east coast.  Along the south coast, the storm surge was 4 to 6 feet and seas from 30 to a little over 35 feet were observed in the outer coastal waters.  The very large waves on top of the storm surge caused destructive coastal flooding along stretches of the Rhode Island exposed south coast.  ||Sandy grew into a hurricane over the southwest Caribbean and then headed north across Jamaica, Cuba, and the Bahamas.  As Sandy headed north of the Bahamas, the storm interacted with a vigorous weather system moving west to east across the United States and began to take on a hybrid structure.  Strong high pressure over southeast Canada helped with the expansion of the strong winds well north of the center of Sandy.  In essence, Sandy retained the structure of a hurricane near its center (until shortly before landfall) while taking on more of an extra-tropical cyclone configuration well away from the center.  Sandy���s track was unusual.  The storm headed northeast and then north across the western Atlantic and then sharply turned to the west to make landfall near Atlantic City, NJ during Monday evening.  Sandy subsequently weakened and moved west across southern Pennsylvania on Tuesday before turning north and heading across western New York state into Quebec during Tuesday night and Wednesday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observing System at Beverly Municipal Airport (KBVY) recorded sustained winds of 46 mph and wind gusts to 59 mph.  A tree was downed onto one lane of Route 1 Southbound in Peabody.  A tree was downed on Route 114 in Danvers.  Large limbs were downed along Story Avenue in Beverly.  Route 62 in Beverly was closed due to multiple downed trees across the road.  On School Street, a tree was downed onto two cars.  In Lynn, a tree was downed onto a house on Washington Avenue.  A tree was downed onto a house on Tremont Street in Peabody.  A tree was downed also onto Northshore Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After thunderstorms occurred during the early morning hours, clouds cleared allowing for ample sunshine.  This resulted in the atmosphere destabilizing.  A cold front moved through the region producing showers and thunderstorms.  Some of these storms became severe producing damaging winds.  High rainfall rates in a moisture laden atmosphere resulted in flash flooding as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Chapin Street and the intersection of East and Birch Streets in Ludlow were flooded with one foot of water.  Chapin Street was closed due to the flooding.  A car was stuck in flood waters at the intersection of East Mountain Road and Route 20 in Westfield.  Portions of Route 10 in Southwick were flooded with six inches of water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through Southern New England bringing showers and thunderstorms to the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 62 year old woman and a 4 year old girl were struck by lightning while getting out of a car.  The woman was holding an umbrella for the girl when lightning struck.  Both were transported to Cape Cod Hospital for evaluation but were released with no injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sandy, a hybrid storm with both tropical and extra-tropical characteristics, brought high winds and coastal flooding to southern New England.  Easterly winds gusted to 50 to 60 mph for interior southern New England; 55 to 65 mph along the eastern Massachusetts coast and along the I-95 corridor in southeast Massachusetts and Rhode Island; and 70 to 80 mph along the southeast Massachusetts and Rhode Island coasts.  A few higher higher gusts occurred along the Rhode Island coast.  A severe thunderstorm embedded in an outer band associated with Sandy produced wind gusts to 90 mph and concentrated damage in Wareham early Tuesday evening, |a day after the center of Sandy had moved into New Jersey.  In general, moderate coastal flooding occurred along the Massachusetts coastline, and major coastal flooding impacted the Rhode Island coastline.  The storm surge was generally 2.5 to 4.5 feet along the east coast of Massachusetts, but peaked late Monday afternoon in between high tide cycles.  Seas built to between 20 and 25 feet Monday afternoon and evening just off the Massachusetts east coast.  Along the south coast, the storm surge was 4 to 6 feet and seas from 30 to a little over 35 feet were observed in the outer coastal waters.  The very large waves on top of the storm surge caused destructive coastal flooding along stretches of the Rhode Island exposed south coast.  ||Sandy grew into a hurricane over the southwest Caribbean and then headed north across Jamaica, Cuba, and the Bahamas. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approaching from the west provided enough lift for showers and thunderstorms. Most damage was the result of strong to damaging winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front with a developing triple point and occlusion resulted in heavy rain and a few embedded thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred on the Boston College campus in Newton.  The lower level of a parking garage flooded, roads on the lower campus were impassable for several hours, |eight vehicles were damaged by flooding, and water damage occurred in 3 buildings (including an ice rink).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary over the area coupled with 1000 to 2000 J/kg of CAPE (instability) to produce a few thunderstorms.  One of these storms became severe and produced some wind damage in Concord.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees on Sudbury Road were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance in the Great Lakes bringing an area of low pressure through New England combined with tropical moisture to produce showers and thunderstorms across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree and wires on Hill Road were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and moist southerly flow of air moved into the region in advance of a powerful cold front. The combination of partial sunshine, increasing amounts of instability and strong wind dynamics with this system placed the region into a rare Moderate Risk by the Storm Prediction Center. Several supercell thunderstorms developed earlier in the day in advance of the main squall line associated with the cold front with the potential for tornadoes, although none developed across the region. The main hazard was straight line damaging wind gusts from a squall line during the afternoon and early evening hours that resulted in numerous power outages and trees down across the area. The squall line moved east of the area later in the evening, ending the threat for severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down due to thunderstorm winds in Dalton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and moist southerly flow of air moved into the region in advance of a powerful cold front. The combination of partial sunshine, increasing amounts of instability and strong wind dynamics with this system placed the region into a rare Moderate Risk by the Storm Prediction Center. Several supercell thunderstorms developed earlier in the day in advance of the main squall line associated with the cold front with the potential for tornadoes, although none developed across the region. The main hazard was straight line damaging wind gusts from a squall line during the afternoon and early evening hours that resulted in numerous power outages and trees down across the area. The squall line moved east of the area later in the evening, ending the threat for severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were reported down due to thunderstorm winds in Sheffield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance in the Great Lakes bringing an area of low pressure through New England combined with tropical moisture to produce showers and thunderstorms across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees, wires, and tree limbs were downed by thunderstorm winds, including on Sheep Pasture Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cove Road was closed due to one foot of water over the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After thunderstorms occurred during the early morning hours, clouds cleared allowing for ample sunshine.  This resulted in the atmosphere destabilizing.  A cold front moved through the region producing showers and thunderstorms.  Some of these storms became severe producing damaging winds.  High rainfall rates in a moisture laden atmosphere resulted in flash flooding as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One lane of Washington Street, between Court and Pleasant Streets, collapsed into a sinkhole seven feet in diameter and eight feet deep.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot and humid conditions resulted in diurnal showers and thunderstorms.  One of these storms became severe, resulting in some wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 29 year old male and a 29 year old female were knocked unconscious when lightning struck them at Thomas Park, where the pair were watching the July 4th fireworks.  They regained consciousness prior to the arrival of EMTs, but unable to feel their legs and were transported to Brigham and Women's Hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving through a very warm, moist airmass over Rhode Island and eastern Massachusetts resulted in showers and thunderstorms. Many of these storms became severe, producing damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Manomet Current reported three waterspouts formed just offshore at 4:06pm EDT and then moved quickly onshore at White Horse Beach, resulting in minor damage.  The damage was minimal.  A window that cranks open was ripped off its hinges and shattered and a beach umbrella and a few awnings were damaged.  Wind speeds were likely less than 65 mph (the minimum wind speed of an EF0 tornado).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across southern New England into a warm moist airmass with plenty of instability.  This resulted in severe thunderstorms and in some cases training of storms that resulted in flash flooding across some of the more urban areas.  A microburst occurred in Arlington, knocking down numerous trees and damaging a roof.  Also, several railroad crossings along the Greenbush and Old Colony commuter rail lines in Massachusetts lost power due to lightning.  This delayed both lines by 20 to 25 minutes as trains slowed down and had crews walking along the train as it crossed the roads at those crossings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A microburst occurred in Arlington, downing numerous trees.  Most of these trees were downed in an approximately 1 square mile section of eastern Arlington, between the intersection of Massachusetts Avenue and Broadway and Route 16 around the Cambridge/Arlington line.  Numerous houses and vehicles were damaged by downed trees.  Traffic signals and street lights were also damaged or downed by falling trees/wind.

A complex and powerful low pressure system moved across the region bringing damaging winds and widespread heavy rainfall. A low pressure system originating in the Deep South approached the region from the southwest during the day as a strong cold front approached from the Great Lakes region. These systems merged and moved across the northeastern United States. Moisture was transported into the region on a very strong low level jet. Rainfall totals ranged from just over an inch up to nearly three inches. Despite instability being very limited, the winds field was so strong aloft that showers and low-topped thunderstorms were able to transport strong, damaging winds to the surface, producing damage to trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down and on a car due to non-thunderstorm winds in Great Barrington.

Also, several railroad crossings along the Greenbush and Old Colony commuter rail lines in Massachusetts lost power due to lightning.  This delayed both lines by 20 to 25 minutes as trains slowed down and had crews walking along the train as it crossed the roads at those crossings.

This delayed both lines by 20 to 25 minutes as trains slowed down and had crews walking along the train as it crossed the roads at those crossings.

A warm front moved north through southern New England producing scattered showers and thunderstorms across a very warm, humid environment.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rowe Elementary School was completely destroyed by fire after being struck by lightning.  Almost 125 firefighters from 19 neighboring towns as well as towns in nearby Vermont helped to fight the fire, but some were delayed by the heavy rain that fell at the same time.  Some firefighters took nearly 45 minutes to reach the scene.  Approximately 70 students attend the elementary school.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through southern New England, resulting in a line of thunderstorms that produced strong to severe winds.  In addition, a strong low level jet produced gusty strong to high winds with the front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed at the intersection of Howe Street and Jarvis Avenue in Hingham.  A tree was downed on Garden Road in Scituate.  An amateur radio operator recorded a wind gust of 47 mph on a home weather station in Hingham.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed in a warm, moist atmosphere.  Cold air aloft contributed to an unstable environment resulting in severe thunderstorms.  Moisture was such that rainfall rates were high enough to result in flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two houses were struck by lightning in Norwood.  One on Lincoln Street, the other on Hawthorne Street.  Minor damage was sustained by both.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved north through southern New England producing scattered showers and thunderstorms across a very warm, humid environment.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 2 was flooded with 2 feet of water.  Firefighters trying to get to the Rowe Elementary School were delayed due to the flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front stalled across southern New England providing a focus for showers and thunderstorms.  Plenty of moisture in the atmosphere led to efficient rainfall rates and flash flooding occurred, mainly in Rhode Island and southeastern Massachusetts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to six inches of rain fell across Fall River, three of which fell in 45 minutes.  Numerous roads and intersections were flooded, stranding many cars.  There were 6 high water rescues, that occurred on Davol and Pearce Streets.  Water was up to car windshields on Pearce Street.  Four feet of water flooded the intersection of Lindsey and Ballard Streets.  Forty to sixty cars became stuck in high water on Eastern Avenue near the rotary with President Avenue.  Airport Road was closed due to flooding.  A daycare center was evacuated because water began entering the building.  More than 50 houses experienced flooding up to or on the first floor.  The basement of Kimwell  Nursing Home on New Boston Road was flooded.  The emergency room at St. Anne's Hospital was flooded.  A massive sinkhole opened up on Crescent Street, taking the cab of one of Fall River's new trash trucks with it.  The North End Senior Center was flooded and closed for several days to repair flood damage.  A newspaper account cited approximately $100,000 to the Sagamore Mill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through southern New England, resulting in a line of thunderstorms that produced strong to severe winds.  In addition, a strong low level jet produced gusty strong to high winds with the front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed on Sikes Avenue in West Springfield.  A tree was downed at the intersection of School and Monroe Streets in Agawam.  Also in Agawam, a tree was downed on Rowley Street.  The Automated Surface Observing Station at Barnes Municipal Airport in Westfield (KBAF) recorded a wind gust of 48 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed within one of the bands rotating through Southern New England from superstorm Sandy.   A strong low level jet, coupled with elevated instability resulted in a couple of these storms becoming severe.  One of the storms produced a significant microburst in Wareham, MA.  With an influx of tropical moisture from Sandy, at least one of these storms also produced flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two and a half feet of water flooded Main Street near Belleville Avenue in New Bedford.  The intersections of Church and Princeton Streets and Ashley Boulevard and Wood Street were flooded with a foot and a half of water.  Purchase Street was flooded with a foot and a half of water.  Phillips Road near Pine Hill Drive was flooded with a foot of water.  A condo complex on Heritage Drive was flooded with a foot of water.  Belleville Road was closed due to two and a half feet of water over the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through southern New England, resulting in a line of thunderstorms that produced strong to severe winds.  In addition, a strong low level jet produced gusty strong to high winds with the front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wires were downed on Labelle Road and a tree and wires were downed on Maxwell Road, both in Charlemont.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed onto Interstate 90 westbound near exit 10 in Agawam.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed on Hyde Park Avenue in Boston.  The Automated Surface Observing Station at Logan International Airport in Boston (KBOS) recorded sustained winds of 38 mph and a wind gust of 49 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed onto wires in Seekonk.  An amateur radio operator recorded wind gusts to 46 mph on their home weather station.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A car was trapped in floodwaters on Independence Avenue in Quincy.  In Weymouth, Fogg Street was flooded with several inches of water at the entrance to the South Shore Emergency Room entrance.  Columbian Street at Nevin Road was flooded with four feet of water.  Bodwell Drive in Avon was flooded with several cars stuck in flood waters.  Several feet of water on Route 37 in Braintree near the Mutual gas station closed the road.  A car was stuck in flood waters at the intersection of Union Street and Grossman Drive.  In Stoughton, three to four inches of water flooded the travel lanes on Route 27.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex and powerful low pressure system moved across the region bringing damaging winds and widespread heavy rainfall. A low pressure system originating in the Deep South approached the region from the southwest during the day as a strong cold front approached from the Great Lakes region. These systems merged and moved across the northeastern United States. Moisture was transported into the region on a very strong low level jet. Rainfall totals ranged from just over an inch up to nearly three inches. Despite instability being very limited, the winds field was so strong aloft that showers and low-topped thunderstorms were able to transport strong, damaging winds to the surface, producing damage to trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down approximately 1 mile northeast of North Adams due to thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approaching from the west provided enough lift for showers and thunderstorms.  As this front was approaching the atmosphere was sufficiently unstable across western Massachusetts, southwestern New Hampshire and western Connecticut for storms to become severe.  Most damage was the result of strong to damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees on Routes 10 and 142 in Bernardston were downed by thunderstorm winds, closing the roads.  A large tree and wires were downed on Route 142 at Wozniak Road in Northfield.  In Leyden, a tree on East Hill Road was downed on wires.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed within one of the bands rotating through Southern New England from superstorm Sandy.   A strong low level jet, coupled with elevated instability resulted in a couple of these storms becoming severe.  One of the storms produced a significant microburst in Wareham, MA.  With an influx of tropical moisture from Sandy, at least one of these storms also produced flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A microburst occurred in Wareham with a thunderstorm associated with a band from superstorm Sandy.  A swath of 60 to 70 mph wind gusts occurred with pockets of 80 to 90 mph gusts.  The hardest hit locations in Wareham were Swifts Beach, Pinehurst, and Onset Bay Marina.  The survey team determined that a damage area of 2 miles in length and 2 miles in width in this area.  Based on radar signatures and eyewitness accounts, thunderstorm wind damage began in Falmouth around 6:25PM, then became more intense over Wareham around 6:40PM.  The survey team determined that approximately 100 trees were significantly damaged or knocked down in Wareham, with many bringing down wires.  The initial downdraft occurred in Swifts Beach then headed north-northeast over Pinehurst toward Narrows Road.  In the Swifts Beach area there were numerous downed trees with one tree impaling the roof of a house on Sherman Street.  Other points of interest were located on Pilgram Avenue, Judson Street, Pine Street, and Swifts Beach Road.  Trees were also downed near the fire department on Main Street in downtown Wareham.  Trees were also downed on Church Avenue at t he Redwood Park Elderly Complex.  In the Pinehurst area of Wareham, downed trees were evident on Franconia Avenue and Pinehurst Drive.  On Narrows Road, a couple of trees were knocked down across the road.  In the Onset Bay Marina area, boats that were stored on a 3 story tier were tilted in their racks.  A sailboat in the marina was tilted 90 degrees with the mast parallel with the water for about 2 minutes before righting itself.  Additionally, 2 boats of unknown size were reportedly sunk in the downdraft.  In the Nanumett Heights section of Point Independence (in Onset), significant tree damage was confirmed on Prospect and Webster Streets.  The survey team and eyewitness accounts observed much of the tree damage to be oriented in a south to north direction, confirming a microburst.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 138 in Raynham was flooded near the Market Basket.  A car was stuck in flood waters on Tremont Street.  The occupants of the car were trapped and had to be rescued.  Weir Street was impassable due to flooding.  Powderhorn Drive was impassable due to 12 to 15 inches of water over the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sandy, a hybrid storm with both tropical and extra-tropical characteristics, brought high winds and coastal flooding to southern New England.  Easterly winds gusted to 50 to 60 mph for interior southern New England; 55 to 65 mph along the eastern Massachusetts coast and along the I-95 corridor in southeast Massachusetts and Rhode Island; and 70 to 80 mph along the southeast Massachusetts and Rhode Island coasts.  A few higher higher gusts occurred along the Rhode Island coast.  A severe thunderstorm embedded in an outer band associated with Sandy produced wind gusts to 90 mph and concentrated damage in Wareham early Tuesday evening, |a day after the center of Sandy had moved into New Jersey.  In general, moderate coastal flooding occurred along the Massachusetts coastline, and major coastal flooding impacted the Rhode Island coastline.  The storm surge was generally 2.5 to 4.5 feet along the east coast of Massachusetts, but peaked late Monday afternoon in between high tide cycles.  Seas built to between 20 and 25 feet Monday afternoon and evening just off the Massachusetts east coast.  Along the south coast, the storm surge was 4 to 6 feet and seas from 30 to a little over 35 feet were observed in the outer coastal waters.  The very large waves on top of the storm surge caused destructive coastal flooding along stretches of the Rhode Island exposed south coast.  ||Sandy grew into a hurricane over the southwest Caribbean and then headed north across Jamaica, Cuba, and the Bahamas.  As Sandy headed north of the Bahamas, the storm interacted with a vigorous weather system moving west to east across the United States and began to take on a hybrid structure.  Strong high pressure over southeast Canada helped with the expansion of the strong winds well north of the center of Sandy.  In essence, Sandy retained the structure of a hurricane near its center (until shortly before landfall) while taking on more of an extra-tropical cyclone configuration well away from the center.  Sandy���s track was unusual.  The storm headed northeast and then north across the western Atlantic and then sharply turned to the west to make landfall near Atlantic City, NJ during Monday evening.  Sandy subsequently weakened and moved west across southern Pennsylvania on Tuesday before turning north and heading across western New York state into Quebec during Tuesday night and Wednesday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Falmouth, water was coming over the bulkhead and into the parking lot at Falmouth Harbor.  Overwash material washed onto the shore roads at Falmouth Harbor.  Surf Drive at the Salt Pond entrance was closed because it was flooded with water and overwash material.  The bike path near Trunk River Beach was undermined and damaged.  Coastal flooding damaged stairs, walkovers, bulkheads, and seawalls along Menauhant Road east of Great Pond Bridge.  In Dennisport, Old Wharf Road was under water.  In Chatham, the lower parking lot at the Chatham Fish Pier was submerged.  Also, Scatteree Road was flooded.  Route 28 at Jackknife Harbor was closed.  In Sandwich, the boardwalk was flooded.  Dewey Lane, Hammond Road, Saltmarsh Street, and Foster Street were all closed.  In Hyannis Port, Dale Avenue was flooded and impassable at the West Beach Club.  The pier at the Hyannis Port Yacht Club was covered with water.  The parking lot at Eugenia Fortes Beach was underwater.  In Orleans, Nauset Beach was closed and Route 28 was closed.  In Barnstable, Craigville Beach Road at Short Beach was flooded with splashover.  Dale Avenue was flooded and impassable.  All this was due to minor to moderate coastal flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Quincy, Quincy Shore Drive northbound and south of Elm Street was closed due to coastal flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a house at the intersection of Mechanic and Hope Streets.  It struck a tree which imploded sending pieces of wood onto the deck.  The lightning then traveled through the yard, creating a 25 foot trench leading to a house and destroying the wooden stairs on the deck and blowing gutters off the house.  A 74 year old woman who lives in the house was taken to the hospital with chest pains.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Fall River, numerous streets were flooded including Plymouth Avenue with 4 feet of water, Davol Street, and Presidents Avenue with 1-2 feet of water.  A car was stuck in the flood waters on Davol Street.  St. Anne's Hospital had to divert emergency traffic due to flooding in the emergency room several inches deep.  Eighteen patients in the ER were moved to a higher floor.  The emergency room reopened at 7:30am after it was cleaned up.

A car drove into two feet of water on Ettrick Street and became stuck.  Two people had to be rescued from the car.  Westgate Drive was flooded with three feet of water, with several cars stuck in the flood waters.

In Bourne, trees were downed on Regency Drive, Sandwich Road, Wings Neck Road, and Shore Road.  In Sandwich, a downed utility pole closed Route 130 between Snake Pond road and Quaker Meeting House Road.

West Beach was completely flooded.  In Dartmouth, Star of the Sea Drive was closed.  In Swansea, Route 6 was closed.  In Fall River, Battleship Cove was flooded.  All of this was due to minor coastal flooding.

The Automated Surface Observing System at New Bedford Regional Airport (KEWB) recorded sustained winds of 43 mph and gusts to 61 mph.  In Acushnet, a tree was downed onto a car on Mendall Road.  Trees were downed on Oak Street and Perry Hill Road.  In Somerset, a large tree was downed onto Regan Road.  In Fall River, a tree was downed onto a house on Herman Street, damaging the roof and kitchen.  A commercial building between Sixth and Troy Streets sustained a partially collapsed roof.  In New Bedford, an air conditioning unit on the second floor of a house on Maplewood Street was blown into the house and a window was blown in.  A tree was downed at the intersection of Brownell and Hawthorn Streets.  A large limb was downed onto Rockdale Avenue near Buttonwood Park.  Parts of a tin roof were blown off a large furniture building on East Rodney French Boulevard.  In Westport, many trees were downed throughout town, including one on Route 88.  Trees and wires were downed on Sanford and Gifford Roads.  In Fairhaven, a tree was downed onto a car on Adams Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed within one of the bands rotating through Southern New England from superstorm Sandy.   A strong low level jet, coupled with elevated instability resulted in a couple of these storms becoming severe.  One of the storms produced a significant microburst in Wareham, MA.  With an influx of tropical moisture from Sandy, at least one of these storms also produced flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Marion, Route 6 and the fire department were flooded.  In Wareham, Route 28 was flooded and impassable in front of the 99 Restaurant and a pharmacy.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Sandy moved northward off the eastern seaboard of the United States during the last week of October 2012. Due to a very strong blocking ridge of high pressure situated over the Atlantic Ocean, the storm turned back to the northwest and rapidly strengthened as it moved toward the mid-Atlantic coast. Although the storm began transitioning into a non-tropical nor'easter storm, it remained an extremely powerful cyclone. As the storm made landfall in southern New Jersey during the evening of October 29th, bands of rain moved across western New England. The rainfall was not excessively heavy and did not cause any flooding, thanks to dry antecedent conditions. Rainfall amounts were generally less than an inch for valley areas with slightly higher amounts across the high terrain.  The highest rainfall amount reported was 1.60 inches in Savoy.||However, strong and gusty winds in association with the storm caused damage to trees and power lines across the region. Although not quite as widespread as areas across southeastern New York and New Jersey, power outages occurred throughout the region.  Wind gusts of 50 to 70 mph were common from the afternoon of the 29th until the early morning hours of the 30th. The highest wind gust reported was 77 mph at Hancock.  A gust to 61 mph was reported at an elevation of 1450 feet near Otis.  Pittsfield Municipal Airport had a gust to 58 mph and Harriman and West Airport in North Adams reported a gust to 54 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down at Butternut Mountain outside Great Barrington due to high winds. A wind gust to 46 mph at an elevation of 662 feet was reported 2 miles north-northwest of Sheffield at 2:30 pm EST.  A wind gust to 61 mph was reported at an elevation of 1450 feet in Otis at 3:23 pm EST. Two large trees were reported down on East Washington Road in Washington.

Although not quite as widespread as areas across southeastern New York and New Jersey, power outages occurred throughout the region.  Wind gusts of 50 to 70 mph were common from the afternoon of the 29th until the early morning hours of the 30th. The highest wind gust reported was 77 mph at Hancock.  A gust to 61 mph was reported at an elevation of 1450 feet near Otis.  Pittsfield Municipal Airport had a gust to 58 mph and Harriman and West Airport in North Adams reported a gust to 54 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were reported down in New Ashford due to high winds.  A wind gust in Hancock was measured at 77 mph at an elevation at 1128 feet at 3:43 pm EST. A tree was down and on a car at Route 7 and Browdy Mountain Road in Lanesborough.  Trees were reported down on Partridge and Oak Hill Roads in Pittsfield due to high winds. Pittsfield Municipal Airport reported a wind gust of 58 mph at 8:19 pm EST.  A tree was reported down on a home on Knox Road in North Adams. Wires were reported down on Liberty Street in North Adams. Harriman and West Airport in North Adams reported a wind gust of 54 mph at 10:05 pm EST. Multiple trees and wires were reported down in Peru. A wind gust of 45 mph was reported at 3:50 pm EST in Peru and 2 miles west-southwest of Sheffield at an elevation of 1894 feet at 3:00 pm EST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and unstable airmass over Southern New England coupled with an approaching cold front to produce showers and thunderstorms.  A very moisture laden atmosphere allowed several inches of rain to fall in a very short period of time resulting in flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to four inches of rain fell in about 2 hours resulting in the overflowing of several brooks.  One of these was Chicopee Brook, which forced the closure of Route 32/Palmer Road.  Three roads were washed out and in need of repairs due to the flooding.  These include Beebe Road, Palmer Road, and Chestnut Street.  Chestnut Street and Beebe Road were closed and Fenton Road was open in only one lane.  In addition, a few basements were flooded.  In total, nine roads were closed but all were reopened to emergency vehicles and local traffic by 6 pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ample low level moisture was in place across the region ahead of a slow moving frontal boundary.  Although skies were cloudy to start the day, breaks in the clouds towards the noon hour allowed for some instability to develop.  With the large amount of moisture in place and an unstable atmosphere, slow moving thunderstorms formed.  Some of these thunderstorms contained gusty winds, which caused damage to trees and power lines.||As thunderstorms with heavy rainfall moved over the same areas repeatedly, flash flooding occurred across portions of Berkshire County.  Some areas received over four inches of rain, with the highest amount being 5.20 inches in Hancock.||Thunderstorm activity waned by evening due to the frontal boundary crossing the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported in North Adams due to heavy rainfall from repeated thunderstorms. Several roads in the downtown portion of the city were closed due to the flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across southern New England into a warm moist airmass with plenty of instability.  This resulted in severe thunderstorms and in some cases training of storms that resulted in flash flooding across some of the more urban areas.  A microburst occurred in Arlington, knocking down numerous trees and damaging a roof.  Also, several railroad crossings along the Greenbush and Old Colony commuter rail lines in Massachusetts lost power due to lightning.  This delayed both lines by 20 to 25 minutes as trains slowed down and had crews walking along the train as it crossed the roads at those crossings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter to lime sized hail fell in Rehoboth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several areas of low pressure traveled along a stationary front stalled across Southern New England producing showers and a few thunderstorms.  In addition, strong southerly flow brought in a very moist airmass with precipitable water values above two inches.  This resulted in very heavy rain falling in these showers and thunderstorms.  Many locations received up to two to three inches in less than an hour.  This resulted in flash flooding, particularly in more urban areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Longmeadow, Bliss Road was flooded and impassable as well as the Big Y parking lot.  A few cars were stuck in the flood waters.  Williams Street from Peoples Bank west to Wilkin Drive was flooded with two feet of water.  Dwight Road was flooded with up to two and a half feet of water.  At points, water was above the hoods of vehicles that tried to drive through the water.  Converse Street was flooded with about 12 inches of water from Burbank Road to Blueberry Hill Road.  Hazardville Road near Maple Road was flooded with 2 feet of water.  Merryweather Road was flooded with nearly 2 feet of water from Williams Street south to Glenbrook Lane.  Numerous cars were stalled out or stuck in water they tried to drive through.  Avondale Road was partially washed out with a sinkhole on the side of the road.  Mill Brook Drive in East Longmeadow was flooded with two feet of water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of upper level disturbances rotated around a vertically stacked low pressure system in the Great Lakes.  These provided a focus for showers and thunderstorms to develop across southern New England.  Southerly winds drew tropical moisture over the area, resulting in very heavy rain in showers and thunderstorms that developed.  In addition, strong winds in the upper levels and 30-40 knots of deep layer shear resulted in wind damage with the strongest of these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County Street in Somerset was closed due to two feet of water over the road from the overflowing of Buffington Brook.  Florida Avenue and Gibbs Street were flooded.  In Swansea, the intersection of Macomber and Puffer Avenues flooded.  Route 6 in front of the Oriental Pearl Restaurant and the parking lot at Swansea Crossing shopping plaza flooded.  Manhole covers on Route 118 were lifted by flood waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and very humid air mass was in place across western Massachusetts.  As a strong cold front approached, severe thunderstorms developed across much of the region.  Strong thunderstorm updrafts allowed for large hail to develop.  Thunderstorms also produced damaging wind gusts, with trees down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three trees were reported down due to strong thunderstorm winds in North Adams.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance and associated cold pool provided enough lift and instability to produce showers and thunderstorms. In addition, there was enough shear to sustain these thunderstorms. These storms produced damaging wind and some large hail across portions of southern New Hampshire and northeastern Massachusetts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees, limbs, and wires on Second Street, Morris Street, Route 114 Eastbound, and Autumn Road were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very warm and humid air mass was in place across the region.  At the surface, a frontal boundary was draped just south of the area across the northern Mid-Atlantic states.   As a shortwave trough moved across upstate New York, severe thunderstorms broke out across western Massachusetts during the early to mid afternoon hours.  Some of these thunderstorms contained hail as large as half dollar size.  Thunderstorms ended by the late afternoon, as a secondary cold front moved across the area, bringing drier and less humid air into the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half-dollar size (measured) hail was reported during a thunderstorm at the New Boston General Store in New Boston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through southern New England setting off showers and thunderstorms.  Lightning strikes caused the most damage, hitting tress, houses, and transformers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A house on Dartmouth Street was struck by lightning and caught fire.  Most of the damage was to the roof.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving through a very warm, moist airmass over Rhode Island and eastern Massachusetts resulted in showers and thunderstorms. Many of these storms became severe, producing damaging winds and large hail.
